verses.flow: The word of YHWH that came to Joel, the son of Pethuel.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The word of YHWH" (dabar YHWH, H1697 + H3068) is the standard prophetic reception formula, marking divine origin and authority. "Came" (hayah, H1961) indicates an active arriving or becoming, not merely passive reception. Joel ben Pethuel is otherwise unknown in Scripture, focusing attention on the message rather than the messenger's lineage.

words.g: and to broaden out the ear
words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur
particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Hear this, O elders, and give ear, all inhabitants of the land! Has this been in your days, or in the days of your fathers?
Tell your children of it, and let your children tell their children, and their children to another generation.

What the cutting locust (gazam) left, the swarming locust (arbeh) has eaten. What the swarming locust left, the hopping locust (yeleq) has eaten. And what the hopping locust left, the destroying locust (hasil) has eaten.
verses.note: HEBREW: Four distinct Hebrew terms for locusts are used: gazam (H1494, "cutter"), arbeh (H697, "swarmer," from rabah, "to multiply"), yeleq (H3218, "licker" or "hopper"), and hasil (H2625, "consumer" or "destroyer"). Whether these denote four species, four life stages of the locust, or a rhetorical fourfold intensification is debated; the cumulative effect emphasizes total devastation. TYPOLOGY: The fourfold locust plague echoes the Exodus plagues (Ex 10:1-20) and anticipates the four horsemen of Revelation 6, suggesting a pattern of escalating divine judgment in redemptive history.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Awake, you drunkards, and weep! And wail, all you drinkers of wine, over the sweet wine (asis), for it is cut off from your mouth.
For a nation has come up against my land — mighty and without number; its teeth are the teeth of a lion, and it has the fangs of a lioness.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The description of the locust army as a "nation" (goy, H1471) without number invites comparison with Joel 2's explicit depiction of YHWH's army. The lion imagery (H738, 'aryeh; H3833, labi') evokes a supernatural destructive power behind the natural catastrophe, consistent with ANE divine warrior traditions where celestial armies manifest in earthly phenomena.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: It has laid waste my vine and splintered my fig tree; it has stripped them bare and cast them down; their branches have been made white.
Wail like a virgin (betulah) girded with sackcloth for the husband of her youth.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Betulah" (H1330) denotes a young woman of marriageable age, most often a virgin. The image of mourning for the "husband of her youth" (ba'al ne'urim) presupposes a betrothed or newly-wed woman — the most acute form of grief in ancient Israelite culture. TYPOLOGY: The mourning bride anticipates the covenant lament motif: Israel as the unfaithful or afflicted bride of YHWH, which is developed in Hosea, Jeremiah, and ultimately resolved in Revelation 21:2.

verses.flow: The grain offering (minchah) and the drink offering are cut off from the house of YHWH. The priests, the ministers of YHWH, mourn.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cessation of the minchah (grain offering, H4503) and nesek (drink offering, H5262) represents a covenantal catastrophe — the daily tamid offering that maintained Israel's ritual standing before YHWH is interrupted. This is not merely economic loss but a rupture in the liturgical life sustaining the covenant relationship. The priests' mourning signals that heaven itself registers the breach.

verses.flow: The field is devastated, the ground mourns; for the grain is devastated, the new wine (tirosh) is dried up, the fresh oil (yitshar) languishes.
Be ashamed, O tillers of the ground; wail, O vinedressers, over the wheat and over the barley, for the harvest of the field has perished.

The vine has dried up and the fig tree languishes — the pomegranate, also the date palm, and the apple tree — all the trees of the field have dried up; for joy (sason) has dried up from the sons of men.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Gird yourselves and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar. Come, spend the night in sackcloth, O ministers of my God, for the grain offering and the drink offering are withheld from the house of your God.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Consecrate a fast (tsom); call a solemn assembly (atsarah); gather the elders and all the inhabitants of the land to the house of YHWH your God, and cry out to YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Atsarah" (H6116), variously translated "solemn assembly" or "sacred convocation," denotes a cultic gathering often associated with the cessation of ordinary labor and collective lamentation or petition. Joel's call to fast and assemble anticipates the fuller call of Joel 2:12-17, where YHWH invites genuine heart-return. TYPOLOGY: This assembly pattern — crisis, gathering, corporate lament, divine response — is paradigmatic for Israel's liturgical theology and is echoed in Nehemiah 9, Jonah 3, and ultimately in the church's practice of communal repentance.

verses.flow: Alas for the day! For the day of YHWH (yom YHWH) is near, and as destruction from the Almighty (Shaddai) it comes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Yom YHWH" (the Day of YHWH) appears here for the first time in Joel and is the book's central theological concept. It is a day of YHWH's active intervention in history — simultaneously judgment and salvation depending on one's covenantal standing. "Shaddai" (H7706, the Almighty) is an archaic divine epithet evoking overwhelming, irresistible power, used deliberately here to stress the inescapable nature of the coming judgment. TYPOLOGY: This verse is foundational to all subsequent Day of YHWH theology in the prophets (Amos 5:18-20, Zeph 1:14-18, Mal 4:5) and finds its NT fulfillment trajectory in the Day of the Lord passages of 1 Thess 5:2, 2 Pet 3:10, and Rev 6:12-17.

verses.flow: Is not the food cut off before our eyes, joy and gladness from the house of our God?

words.g: to pull down or in pieces

verses.flow: The seeds shrivel under their clods; the storehouses are desolate; the granaries are torn down, for the grain has dried up.

words.g: the a collective name for a flock

verses.flow: How the beasts groan! The herds of cattle are in confusion because there is no pasture for them; even the flocks of sheep suffer punishment.
To you, O YHWH, I cry. For fire has devoured the pastures of the wilderness, and flame has burned all the trees of the field.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophet's sudden first-person address to YHWH ("to you, O YHWH, I cry") marks a liturgical pivot from proclamation to petition, modeling the response the entire community is called to make in 2:12-17. The intercessory voice of the prophet on behalf of creation — beast, land, and people — anticipates the priestly-prophetic role of Christ who stands as advocate before the Father (1 John 2:1). HEBREW: "Devoured" (akalah, H398) and "burned" (lahatah, H3857) suggest both literal fire (possibly drought-induced) and the consuming presence of divine judgment, a dual register common in prophetic poetry.

verses.flow: Even the beasts of the field pant (ta'arog) toward you, for the water brooks are dried up, and fire has devoured the pastures of the wilderness.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ta'arog" (H6165) means "to long for" or "to pant after" with intense yearning, the same root used in Psalm 42:1 ("As the deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God"). The use of this term for the beasts' longing toward YHWH creates a profound theological irony: even creation instinctively cries out to its Creator while the human community must be summoned to do so. THEOLOGICAL: This verse closes the chapter with a note of creation's solidarity in suffering (Rom 8:19-22), pointing forward to the eschatological restoration where the whole created order is renewed under YHWH's reign.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur

words.g: in/with a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Blow the trumpet (shofar) in Zion, and sound an alarm in my holy mountain! Let all the inhabitants of the land tremble, for the day of YHWH is coming — for it is near —
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shofar" (Strong's H7782) is the ram's horn trumpet, the covenantal alarm instrument. "My holy mountain" identifies Zion as YHWH's cosmic dwelling, echoing the divine council's mountain of assembly (Isa 14:13). The "day of YHWH" (yom YHWH) is the foundational eschatological concept of Joel, denoting YHWH's direct intervention in history as divine warrior and judge. THEOLOGICAL: The trumpet blast as alarm signals the reversal of the priestly blessing function (Num 10:9); Zion, typically the place of salvation proclamation, here becomes the platform for a terror announcement, underscoring the gravity of the approaching judgment.

words.g: the a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: a day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness, like the dawn spread upon the mountains — a great and mighty people; there has not been the like from of old, and after them none will be again through the years of generation upon generation.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of an invading locust army (or metaphorical military force) advancing like "dawn spread upon the mountains" reflects ANE battle descriptions. The incomparability formula ("there has not been the like") echoes the Exodus plagues (Exod 10:14), deliberately casting this judgment as a new, surpassing Exodus-of-wrath. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "generation upon generation" (dor va-dor) emphasizes the unprecedented and final character of this judgment, framing it within the eschatological telos of history.

verses.flow: Before them fire devours, and behind them a flame burns; before them the land is like the garden of Eden, and behind them a desolate wilderness — and also no survivor escapes them.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The before/after contrast — Eden to wilderness — inverts the creation and redemption narrative. Eden represents the state of divine blessing (Gen 2), while the ensuing desolation pictures de-creation. This anticipates the ultimate reversal: the new creation in Christ restoring Eden (Rev 22:1-5). The motif of no survivor foreshadows the absolute nature of the final Day of YHWH judgment.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Their appearance is like the appearance of horses, and like war horses so they run.

words.g: the a mountain or range of hills

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Like the rumble of chariots on the tops of the mountains they leap, like the crackling sound of a flame of fire consuming stubble, like a mighty people arrayed for battle.
Before them peoples are in anguish; all faces grow pale.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Like mighty warriors they run, like men of war they scale the wall; each one marches in his own path, and they do not swerve from their ways.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

words.g: and in up to or over against

verses.flow: Each does not crowd his brother; each one goes in his own track. And through the weapons they fall, yet they do not cut short their advance.

words.g: in/with + in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

words.g: in up to or over against

verses.flow: They rush upon the city, they run upon the wall; they climb up into the houses, they enter through the windows like a thief.
Before them the earth quakes, the heavens tremble; the sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: And YHWH utters his voice before his army, for his camp is exceedingly great; for mighty is the one who executes his word. For the day of YHWH is great and very awesome — and who can endure it?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's "army" (chayil) here is his heavenly host executing his sovereign decree. The motif of YHWH commanding a celestial/cosmic army before whom the earth trembles is rooted in divine council theology (Ps 103:20-21; Isa 13:3-5). "Who can endure it?" is a rhetorical question implying that no unaided human being can stand before the divine warrior — only those granted covenant standing survive. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's voice (qol) preceding the army recalls the theophanic thunderstorm imagery of Ps 29, where YHWH's voice shakes creation.

verses.flow: "Yet even now," declares YHWH, "return to me with all your heart, with fasting and with weeping and with mourning;"

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: and rend your hearts and not your garments. And return to YHWH your God, for he is gracious and merciful (rachum), slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love (hesed), and he relents over disaster.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rachum" (Strong's H7349) — merciful, from the root rechem (womb), connoting deep, maternal compassion. "Hesed" (Strong's H2617) — steadfast covenant love, loyalty, lovingkindness; untranslatable by a single English word. This verse quotes the divine self-disclosure of Exod 34:6-7, the foundational covenant character formula repeated throughout the OT (Ps 86:15; Jon 4:2; Neh 9:17). THEOLOGICAL: The call to rend hearts rather than garments is a radical internalization of covenant repentance, anticipating the new covenant of the heart (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:26).

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Who knows whether he will not turn and relent, and leave behind him a blessing — a grain offering and a drink offering for YHWH your God?
Blow the trumpet (shofar) in Zion; sanctify a fast; call a solemn assembly;

gather the people, sanctify the congregation, assemble the elders, gather the children and the nursing infants. Let the bridegroom come forth from his chamber, and the bride from her wedding canopy (chuppah).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur
verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests weep, the ministers of YHWH, and let them say: "Spare your people, O YHWH, and do not make your inheritance a reproach, a byword among the nations. Why should they say among the peoples, 'Where is their God?'"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The location "between the vestibule and the altar" is the precise inner court space of the Jerusalem Temple, making this a formal liturgical lament. This is the space where priests ministered daily, now transformed into a place of communal grief. THEOLOGICAL: The petition "Why should they say among the peoples, 'Where is their God?'" is a covenant honor argument — the nations' mockery impugns YHWH's name and reputation (cf. Ps 79:10; Ezek 36:20-23), which becomes the grounds for YHWH's response of salvation.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then YHWH became jealous for his land and had pity on his people.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: And YHWH answered and said to his people: "Behold, I am sending to you grain and new wine and fresh oil, and you will be satisfied with them; and I will no more make you a reproach among the nations."

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing + suffix

conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: "And the northerner I will remove far from you, and will drive him into a land parched and desolate, his face toward the eastern sea and his rear toward the western sea; and his stench will rise up, and his foul smell will ascend, for he has done great things."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: Fear not, O land; rejoice and be glad, for YHWH has done great things!
Fear not, O beasts of the field, for the pastures of the wilderness are green; for the tree bears its fruit, the fig tree and the vine yield their full strength.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in YHWH your God; for he has given you the early rain (moreh) for righteousness, and he has poured down for you abundant rain — the early and the latter rain (malkosh) as before.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Moreh" (Strong's H4175) is disputed — it can mean "early rain" or "teacher for righteousness." The rabbis and some scholars read this as a messianic teacher of righteousness, while the agricultural reading (early rain) fits the immediate context. The dual meaning is likely intentional, foreshadowing both physical restoration and spiritual instruction. The "latter rain" (malkosh) refers to the spring rains critical for the final ripening of the harvest. TYPOLOGY: The outpouring of rain anticipates the Spirit's outpouring in v. 28; water and Spirit are linked in new covenant theology (Isa 44:3; John 7:37-39).

words.g: and to fill or be full of
words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: and to run after or over
words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: And the threshing floors shall be full of grain, and the vats shall overflow with new wine and fresh oil.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 1st common sing

words.g: and + the a kind of locust

verses.flow: "I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten — the hopper, the destroyer, and the cutter — my great army which I sent among you."
verses.note: HEBREW: Four distinct Hebrew terms for the locust are used across Joel 1-2: gazam (cutter/gnawer), arbeh (swarming locust, the most common term), yelek (hopper/licker), and chasil (destroyer/consumer). Scholars debate whether these are different species or different life stages of the locust. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's identification of the locusts as "my great army which I sent" reframes natural disaster as divine instrument, a key theological move asserting YHWH's absolute sovereignty over creation and history.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: "And you shall eat and be satisfied, and praise the name of YHWH your God, who has dealt wondrously with you. And my people shall not be put to shame forever."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: "And you shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am YHWH your God and there is no other; and my people shall not be put to shame forever."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes (shebut) of Judah and Jerusalem,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Restore the fortunes" renders shebut (שְׁבוּת, Strong's H7622), a term meaning "captivity" or "turning of fortune," suggesting both a return from exile and a comprehensive covenantal restoration. THEOLOGICAL: This verse opens the eschatological section of Joel and frames the cosmic judgment of chapter 3 as inseparable from YHWH's redemptive purpose for his people, establishing the Day of YHWH as simultaneously a day of salvation for Israel and of judgment for the nations.

verses.flow: I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat. And I will enter into judgment with them there, on account of my people and my inheritance Israel, whom they have scattered among the nations; and my land they have divided.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Valley of Jehoshaphat" (יְהוֹשָׁפָט, "YHWH judges") is not a firmly identified geographical location but a theological toponym — the place where YHWH renders his sovereign verdict over the nations. THEOLOGICAL: The assembly of all nations before YHWH for judicial sentencing reflects the divine council imagery in which YHWH presides as supreme Judge (Ps 82:1, Dan 7:9-10). The indictment centers on the scattering of Israel and the partition of YHWH's land (eretz), asserting his proprietary claim over both people and territory.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

words.g: blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: And for my people they have cast lots, and have given a boy in exchange for a prostitute, and a girl they sold for wine, and they drank.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The selling of children for a prostitute's fee or a skin of wine reflects ancient Near Eastern practices of debt-slavery and war-captivity trafficking. THEOLOGICAL: The casual commodification of human persons made in the image of God (imago Dei) intensifies the moral indictment; YHWH takes personal offense at the mistreatment of his covenant people as though they were worthless chattel.

words.g: to/for blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: And also, what are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia? Are you repaying me a recompense? And if you are repaying me, swiftly, speedily will I return your recompense upon your own head.
